name: Inversion
description: Inversion teaches decision quality by starting from failure cases instead of success fantasies. Learners build stronger judgment when they can name what breaks systems before they choose what to build. This framework is especially useful for high-stakes choices where one preventable error can erase many good moves.
mechanics: Begin by writing the target outcome with a measurable result and a deadline so your analysis has boundaries. Flip the question and ask, "If this fails badly, what most likely caused it?" then capture concrete failure modes. Separate those failures into categories like assumptions, execution gaps, dependency risks, and human coordination mistakes. Turn each high-risk failure mode into a preventive control such as a checklist item, gating criterion, or rollback trigger. Run a short red-team exercise where someone argues against the plan using evidence from past incidents or comparable projects. Prioritize mitigations with a simple impact-by-likelihood score and address the top tier before launch. During execution, revisit the inversion list at milestones to catch newly emerging risks while they are still cheap to fix. End with a reflection that compares predicted failures to real outcomes so your future inversion quality improves over time.
application: Use inversion at the start of each major initiative by requiring a one-page "failure map" before approving resources.
flavor: "When heroes chase glory, sages study ruins."

name: Second-Order Thinking
description: Second-Order Thinking trains you to evaluate decisions by downstream effects, not just immediate wins. It helps learners see how incentives, behavior, and system constraints shift after the first move. The framework creates strategic depth by revealing outcomes that are invisible to first-order analysis.
mechanics: Define each option and state its immediate result in one sentence so first-order effects are explicit. Ask what each immediate result changes in behavior, incentives, and resource flow across stakeholders. Map likely second-order effects on a timeline to distinguish short-term discomfort from long-term value. Extend to third-order effects only for high-impact choices so analysis remains proportional to stakes. Identify adaptation loops by asking how competitors, teammates, or users will respond once your action becomes visible. Attach leading indicators to your map so you can observe whether predicted downstream effects are appearing. Pre-plan countermeasures for the two most damaging side effects before committing to the decision. After implementation, compare predicted chains with actual outcomes and update your strategic heuristics.
application: When deciding between alternatives, document one expected effect at 30 days, 90 days, and 12 months before choosing.
flavor: "The novice counts the first wave; the strategist reads the tides behind it."

name: Five Whys
description: Five Whys develops diagnostic discipline by tracing symptoms to structural causes. It teaches teams to replace blame and guesswork with evidence-linked reasoning. The method stays simple on purpose, making it easy to teach and repeat across incidents.
mechanics: Start with a specific, observable problem statement that includes time, place, and impact. Ask the first why and answer using verifiable data such as logs, transcripts, screenshots, or direct observation notes. Continue asking why, ensuring each answer causes the next question rather than branching into unrelated narratives. Stop only when you reach a systemic and actionable cause that your team can directly influence. Validate the proposed root cause by checking whether fixing it would prevent similar failures in adjacent scenarios. If any link in the chain is speculative, pause the analysis and gather additional evidence before finalizing conclusions. Convert the root cause into one or two corrective actions with explicit owners, due dates, and success metrics. Revisit the problem over subsequent cycles to confirm recurrence declines, proving you treated cause instead of symptom.
application: In post-incident reviews, require each why-answer pair to cite one artifact so the chain remains evidence-based.
flavor: "Each why is a torch deeper into the mine."

name: Kaizen
description: Kaizen creates a practical learning culture through frequent, low-risk process experiments. Instead of waiting for a big transformation, learners build capability by improving one repeatable step at a time. Over months, these micro-gains compound into major performance and quality improvements.
mechanics: Select one recurring workflow and establish a baseline metric such as lead time, defect rate, or rework frequency. Break the workflow into stages and mark friction points where delays, confusion, or handoff failures occur. Form a small improvement hypothesis that can be tested within days rather than weeks. Implement one change at a time so causal impact stays clear and measurement remains interpretable. Track before-and-after data in a visible log that includes what changed, who changed it, and what result followed. Standardize improvements that work by updating checklists, SOP snippets, or templates used by the team. Invite frontline contributors to propose the next test because they observe process pain in real time. Repeat this cycle continuously, treating each iteration as a short learning sprint that strengthens both results and team judgment.
application: Run a weekly 20-minute Kaizen standup where each member presents one completed experiment and one next hypothesis.
flavor: "A thousand tiny sharpenings can split a mountain."

name: Swipe File
description: A Swipe File supports skill development by turning great examples into reusable creative patterns. Learners avoid blank-page paralysis because they can study proven structures before drafting. Its real value comes from analysis and adaptation, not copying finished work.
mechanics: Collect strong examples across channels and keep only entries with clear outcomes or demonstrated audience response. Annotate each example with context fields like audience, objective, format, and why the execution likely performed well. Tag patterns such as opening hook, narrative arc, offer framing, visual layout, or objection handling so retrieval is fast. During planning, select several relevant references and dissect how each one handles attention, trust, and action. Extract the transferable moves and rebuild them with your own voice, constraints, and product truth. Draft quickly using the extracted pattern skeleton, then revise for originality and contextual fit. After publishing, record performance metrics and add your own successful work back into the library. Prune stale entries quarterly so your swipe file reflects current audience expectations and platform norms.
application: Before creating content, review three references and note one pattern from each that you will adapt in your own draft.
flavor: "The archive does not steal fire; it teaches where sparks survive."

name: First Principles
description: First Principles reasoning teaches you to rebuild solutions from foundational truths instead of inherited assumptions. It is powerful when conventional practices are expensive, slow, or poorly justified. Learners gain breakthrough potential by distinguishing what is truly constrained from what is merely customary.
mechanics: Define the problem with precision by naming the objective, hard constraints, and acceptable tradeoffs. List all assumptions currently guiding your approach, especially statements accepted without evidence. Decompose the problem into primitive truths grounded in physics, math, economics, or operational reality. Test each assumption against those truths and discard assumptions that fail causal scrutiny. Reconstruct a fresh solution using only validated truths plus explicit tradeoff decisions. Compare the rebuilt approach with status-quo options for cost, speed, quality, and risk exposure. Prototype the highest-leverage difference early to validate that theory survives real-world constraints. Capture which assumptions changed and why so the team can reuse the learning in adjacent decisions.
application: When you hear "that is how we always do it," pause and verify whether the claimed constraint is physical, legal, or just habitual.
flavor: "When old maps fail, return to the bedrock and redraw the world."

name: Pareto Principle
description: The Pareto Principle helps learners identify the minority of actions that produce most measurable results. It prevents diluted effort by making prioritization data-driven rather than intuitive. Used consistently, it improves throughput without increasing total workload.
mechanics: Choose a measurable outcome such as revenue growth, churn reduction, error prevention, or cycle-time improvement. List contributing inputs and collect impact data for each over the same observation period. Rank inputs by contribution and calculate cumulative percentages to reveal concentration patterns. Identify the vital few contributors generating the dominant share of the outcome, even when the split is not exactly 80/20. Reallocate attention, budget, and execution capacity toward those high-leverage inputs first. Reduce, automate, or delegate low-yield activities that consume disproportionate effort. Recompute the distribution on a regular cadence because leverage shifts as products, teams, and markets evolve. Pair Pareto focus with quality guardrails so short-term optimization does not erode trust or long-term resilience.
application: At month-end, create an impact ranking of your work and intentionally remove at least one low-yield recurring task.
flavor: "Master the vital few, and the trivial many lose their grip."

name: OODA Loop
description: OODA Loop builds execution agility through fast cycles of Observe, Orient, Decide, and Act. Learners improve by shortening feedback loops rather than waiting for certainty. The framework rewards teams that update mental models quickly as conditions change.
mechanics: Observe by collecting fresh signals from customers, systems, competitors, and internal operations. Orient by interpreting those signals through context, goals, constraints, and known biases. Decide on a near-term action that advances the mission while preserving flexibility for the next cycle. Act quickly with a bounded move, experiment, or operational adjustment rather than a large irreversible commitment. Capture immediate feedback and feed it into the next observation phase without delay. Reduce loop time by clarifying decision rights and removing slow approval bottlenecks. Maintain explicit success and stop criteria so speed does not degrade decision quality. Run periodic after-action reviews to improve orientation accuracy and sharpen future decision heuristics.
application: For fast projects, hold a 10-minute daily OODA huddle to update assumptions, choose one decisive action, and define a feedback signal.
flavor: "In the storm, victory belongs to the one who loops faster."

name: Jobs to Be Done
description: Jobs to Be Done teaches product thinking through the progress users seek in real situations. It moves learners beyond demographic assumptions to context, motivation, and friction. Teams build better products when they design for the job users are trying to complete, not just requested features.
mechanics: Interview users about specific moments when they switched tools, delayed action, or improvised workarounds. Define the core job and separate its functional, emotional, and social dimensions. Map the journey from trigger to successful completion, highlighting friction, uncertainty, and failure points. Analyze all competing alternatives, including spreadsheets, manual hacks, and doing nothing, to understand true competition. Score opportunities by job importance and current satisfaction to find high-value innovation zones. Design features and onboarding around the critical moments that unblock progress in the job journey. Validate with behavioral evidence such as activation quality, retention movement, and switch intent rather than stated preference alone. Revisit job definitions as markets and user contexts evolve so product strategy stays aligned with real progress needs.
application: In discovery calls, ask what users were trying to accomplish before discussing desired features or UI preferences.
flavor: "Hear not the request alone—hear the quest beneath it."

name: Pre-Mortem
description: A Pre-Mortem improves planning by assuming failure in advance and learning from that imagined outcome. It counters optimism bias while there is still time to change course. Learners gain risk literacy by practicing structured anticipation rather than reactive firefighting.
mechanics: Assemble key stakeholders before execution and clarify scope, goals, success criteria, and critical dependencies. Ask everyone to imagine a concrete future date when the initiative has failed significantly. Have participants write independent failure narratives first so dominant voices do not anchor the group too early. Cluster risks into themes such as strategy mismatch, execution gaps, staffing, external dependencies, and communication breakdowns. Score each risk by probability and impact, then select the highest-priority set for immediate mitigation design. Convert mitigations into explicit owners, early warning indicators, and contingency actions embedded in the delivery plan. Re-run abbreviated pre-mortems at milestone checkpoints because risk profiles shift as reality unfolds. Close with a post-project comparison of predicted versus actual failures to improve future forecasting and planning discipline.
application: Before kickoff, run a 30-minute pre-mortem and require every top risk to include both a detection signal and a named response owner.
flavor: "Name the ghosts before the battle, and fewer will rise."
